questions.text: What was the strange rule in the Kingdom of Fools?
options: People had to walk on their hands
They slept during the day and worked at night
No one was allowed to speak
questions.explanation: The king and minister ordered that day be treated as night and night as day.

questions.text: What was the price of any item in the Kingdom of Fools?
questions.explanation: Everything, whether a measure of rice or a bunch of bananas, cost a single duddu.

questions.text: Who arrived in the city one day?
questions.explanation: A wise Guru and his disciple arrived and were surprised by the city's rules.

questions.text: Why did the Guru decide to leave the city?
options: He realized it was a kingdom of fools and dangerous
The king ordered him to leave
questions.explanation: He knew that the behavior of fools is unpredictable and staying there was risky.

questions.text: Why did the disciple decide to stay?
options: He wanted to be the minister
He had no money to travel
questions.explanation: The disciple was a gourmet and wanted to enjoy the cheap and plenty food.

questions.text: Whose house did the thief break into?
questions.explanation: A thief broke into a rich merchant’s house by making a hole in the wall.

questions.text: How did the thief die?
options: The wall of the house collapsed on him
questions.explanation: As he was carrying out his loot, the wall of the old house collapsed on his head.

questions.text: Whom did the King summon first for the thief's death?
questions.explanation: The merchant was summoned because it was his house and his 'weak' wall.

questions.text: The merchant blamed the ______ for the weak wall.
questions.explanation: He argued that he didn't build the wall; the man who built it was responsible.

questions.text: Why did the bricklayer say he couldn't build a good wall?
questions.explanation: He claimed a girl with jingling anklets kept going up and down the street, distracting him.

questions.text: The dancing girl blamed the ______ for her walking up and down.
questions.explanation: She said she had given some gold to a goldsmith to make jewellery and he delayed it.

questions.text: The goldsmith blamed the ______ for making him work late.
questions.explanation: He said a rich merchant had a wedding and insisted on his orders being finished first.

questions.text: Who was the rich merchant who finally received the death sentence?
options: The owner of the house (same as the first merchant)
questions.explanation: The trail came back to the original merchant whose wall fell.

questions.text: Why could the merchant NOT be executed on the new stake?
options: He was too thin to fit the stake
questions.explanation: The merchant was too thin, and the stake was made for a fat man.

questions.text: Who was chosen for execution instead of the merchant?
questions.explanation: The king's men searched for a fat man and found the disciple, who had grown fat on cheap food.

questions.text: How did the disciple call his Guru?
questions.explanation: The Guru had magic powers and saw everything; he heard the disciple's prayer.

questions.text: What did the Guru whisper to the King?
options: That whoever dies on the stake first will be the next King
questions.explanation: The Guru used a clever trick, claiming the stake was the stake of the god of justice.

questions.text: Who died on the stake at the end?
questions.explanation: Being fools, the King and Minister went to the prison at night and took the place of the Guru and disciple.

questions.text: What did the people do after the King's death?
options: They made the Guru and disciple the new King and Minister
questions.explanation: The people begged the Guru and disciple to rule them.

questions.text: What was the first thing the new rulers changed?
options: They made night as night and day as day again
They changed the price of food
questions.explanation: They restored the natural cycle of time and changed the fixed pricing.

questions.text: In the Kingdom of Fools, even ______ were taught to sleep during the day.
questions.explanation: Even the animals were trained to sleep during the day to avoid being caught.

questions.text: What happened if anyone disobeyed the 'day-night' rule?
questions.explanation: The command was strict, and death was the penalty for disobedience.

questions.text: The Guru said 'Fools are ______.'
questions.explanation: The Guru's wisdom was that one cannot predict what fools will do next.

questions.text: How did the disciple spend his time in the city?
options: Eating bananas, ghee, rice and wheat
questions.explanation: He enjoyed the cheap food and became 'fat like a street-side sacred bull'.

questions.text: The merchant's father was the real ______ according to the King.
questions.explanation: The King argued that the merchant inherited his father's sins along with his riches.

questions.text: What is the moral of the story?
options: Stay away from fools and use wisdom to handle them
questions.explanation: The story highlights that wisdom is required to survive in a world of irrational people.

questions.text: The story is a ______ folk tale.
questions.explanation: It is a Kannada folk tale from A.K. Ramanujan’s 'Folk Tales from India'.

questions.text: Why did the King and Minister NOT want to lose their kingdom in the next life?
options: They were greedy and foolishly believed the Guru
questions.explanation: They fell for the Guru's trick and wanted to be reborn as King and Minister.

questions.text: How did the Guru and disciple escape execution?
options: The Guru used his wit to trick the King
questions.explanation: The Guru's 'sacrificial' trick led the King to execute himself.

questions.text: The word 'scoundrel' was used by the King for:
questions.explanation: He called the merchant a scoundrel for the death of the thief.

questions.text: Why did the Guru want to be executed first?
options: As part of the plan to trick the King
questions.explanation: He pretended to compete with the disciple to make the 'rebirth as king' claim believable.

questions.text: What was the 'duddu'?
questions.explanation: Duddu is the currency used in the kingdom.

questions.text: The people were 'thrown into ______' when they saw the dead bodies of King and Minister.
questions.explanation: They were shocked to see their rulers dead instead of the strangers.

questions.text: Did the Guru agree to be King immediately?
questions.explanation: He agreed only on the condition that they could change all the old laws.

questions.text: What kind of king was the previous ruler?
questions.explanation: The entire story revolves around his foolish and illogical decisions.

questions.text: The merchant's house wall was described as:
questions.explanation: The wall was old and collapsed easily under pressure.

questions.text: The dancing girl was now an '______ woman'.
questions.explanation: Many years had passed since the wall was built, so she was now old.

questions.text: How did the King feel about 'justice'?
options: He wanted it done at any cost, even if illogical
questions.explanation: His sense of justice was perverse and focused on finding someone to kill for the 'crime'.

questions.text: What were the King and Minister worried about after hearing the Guru?
options: Losing their kingdom in the next life
questions.explanation: They didn't want to give their kingdom to anyone else in their next birth.

questions.text: The stake was called the 'stake of the ______'.
questions.explanation: The Guru gave it this name to make it sound holy and desirable.
